name: The roster of the returned exiles
notes: Ezra 2 and Nehemiah 7 are two reckonings of one interlinked people: those who returned from the Babylonian captivity with Zerubbabel, Jeshua, and their fellow leaders, counted family by family, town by town, and order by order -- the people of Israel, the men of the towns, the priests, the Levites, the singers, the porters, the Nethinims and Solomon's servants, those who could not show their genealogy, the grand totals, the beasts, the freewill gifts, and the settling of all Israel in their cities. Both lists openly mix two ways of counting the same households -- by ancestor ('the children of Parosh') and by town ('the men of Bethlehem') -- so a single family in this densely intermarried web can be tallied under one head in one census and under another in the other, the same people partitioned along a different seam. Nehemiah moreover says he found and copied an existing register (Neh 7:5), so the two may also be snapshots taken at different moments -- those who enrolled to go, and the document as it later stood. The clearest sign that one people stands behind both is that they reach the same grand total, forty and two thousand three hundred and threescore: where two records agree on the whole while differing in the parts, it is one group seen through two lenses, not two groups. The differing family tallies, the alternate clan names (Bani / Binnui, Jorah / Hariph, Gibbar / Gibeon), and the few entries kept in one list and not the other are accordingly linked here by name regardless of the count, so the reader can weigh them side by side -- the variations left, as ever, as a matter for further study.

These sought their register among those that were reckoned by genealogy ... put from the priesthood
the Tirshatha said unto them ... till there stood up a priest with Urim and ... Thummim
The whole congregation together was forty and two thousand three hundred and threescore
seven thousand three hundred thirty and seven ... singing men and singing women
horses were seven hundred thirty and six their mules two hundred forty and five
camels four hundred thirty and five ... six thousand seven hundred and twenty
some of the chief of the fathers ... gave
